title: Documentation
text:
Preface
This is a Quarto book.
To learn more about Quarto books visit https://quarto.org/docs/books.

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
text:
1.1 Background
Last Revised: October 10, 2024
VERSION 1.0
This document provides information about the ABC-DS study and documentation of data currently available on the LONI website. The initial ABC-DS study began in 2015. Funded by the National Institutes on Health (NIH) through the U01 mechanism, from 2015-2020, ABC-DS was the combined/harmonized effort of two groups of investigators: 1) the Alzheimer’s Dementia in Down Syndrome (ADDS) group (Columbia University/NYS Institute for Basic Research in Developmental Disabilities, Massachusetts General Hospital, University of California Irvine); 2) the Neurodegeneration in Aging Down Syndrome (NiAD) group (University of Pittsburgh, University of Wisconsin Madison, University of Cambridge, Banner Institute, Washington University).
From 2020-2026, the ABC-DS study was funded by NIH through the U19 mechanism. The number of participating institutions increased to ten (Columbia University/NYS Institute for Basic Research in Developmental Disabilities, Massachusetts General Hospital, University of California Irvine, University of Pittsburgh, University of Wisconsin Madison, University of Cambridge, Washington University, Kansas University Medical Center, and the University of Puerto Rico). A single, harmonized protocol, including many of the ADDS and NiAD measures, was adopted by all ten institutions. The U19 project includes new participants as well as individuals continuing from the U01 projects.
While the focus of ABC-DS is on individuals with Down Syndrome, NiAD also recruited sibling controls, and unaffected siblings continue to be recruited in the U19. In the ongoing U19 study, in addition to new participants, as many former U01 participants as possible have been recruited, for continued evaluation.
ABC-DS participants are scheduled to be seen every 16 months. Sibling controls receive a shorter cognitive evaluation battery than participants with Down Syndrome and do not receive caregiver assessments.

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
text:
1.2 Available Data
ABC-DS data has been harmonized and made available to the research community through the Laboratory of Neuroimaging website (LONI). Cumulative data released at annual intervals include evaluations in which a consensus diagnosis has been generated as of a set date, based on a defined battery of tests. Currently, data on a total of 1,274 evaluations conducted on 503 participants with Down Syndrome (cases) are available on LONI; on 503 individuals with Down Syndrome and 59 sibling controls, along with 161 evaluations conducted on 59 sibling controls. Case and control data are provided in separate tables.
Data collected at multiple evaluations are organized in a one-record-per-evaluation format sorted by participant ID, subject label, and consecutive evaluation (event_sequence), regardless of study of recruitment. For example, a participant with three evaluations in the U01 and one evaluation in the U19 would have data for event_sequence 1, 2, 3 and 4 (the first U19 evaluation). A participant seen in the first and third U01 evaluation with one evaluation in the U19 would have data for event_sequence 1,2 and 3. Note that event_sequence is numbered consecutively, regardless of whether a particular 16 month month evaluation (in this case the second U01 evaluation) is missed. The age_at_event_and_latency table provides the age of the participant at each evaluation and the latency in days from baseline of each subsequent evaluation. The number of evaluations for which data are currently available is shown in Table 1. Please note that the N of evaluations available for any specific measure is affected by a number of factors (please see descriptions of individual measures, below).
Table 1. Number of available ABC-DS evaluations


Evaluation (event_sequence)Down Syndrome CasesSibling Controls1st (baseline)503592nd evaluation346353rd evaluation283174th evaluation13855th evaluation41Total evaluations1,274116

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
text:
1.3 ABC-DS Cognitive & Caregiver Measures (2015-2026)
Some measures in the harmonized data available on LONI were collected in the U19 study, but not in one or both of the U01studies (ADDS and/or NiAD); for the tables containing these measures (flagged in the descriptions below), sample sizes will be smaller than for measures administered in all three studies.

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
section: 1.4 Measures in the ABC-DS Integrated Dataset
text:
1.4 Measures in the ABC-DS Integrated Dataset

1.4.1 Cognitive Assessment Measures
For all the cognitive measures, before performing any modelling, please check the distributions of the variables. The vast majority of them have a skewed distributions and are zero inflated, which requires either some type of transformation or proper modelling.

The Down Syndrome Mental Status Examination (DSMSE) (an enhanced version)[1] provides an omnibus measure of neuropsychological functioning, assessing recall of personal information, orientation, immediate and delayed memory, language, visuospatial function and praxis.
The Rapid Assessment for Developmental Disabilities (RADD)[2] Assesses the ability of the individual to copy simple hand movements. For ABC-DS we are only using the Hand Movements and Imitation Subtest of the measure.2
The McCarthy Scales of Children’s Abilities: Category Fluency Test[3] An adaptation of the McCarthy Category Fluency Test has been employed to assess changes in language ability and frontal lobe function over time. It requires participants to name as many foods and animals as they can within 20 seconds. We have been using a single summary score generated by adding the number of correct responses for each category.2
The NEPSY (2nd ed.) Word Generation Semantic Fluency Subtest[3] assesses verbal productivity through the ability to generate as many words as possible in 20 sec. within a specific semantic category (e.g., animals, food and drinks). The score is the total number of unique animal names/foods/drinks generated.2
Kaufman Brief Intelligence Test (2nd ed)[4] The KBIT-2 is a brief measure of verbal and nonverbal intelligence for ages 4-90. It provides crystallized (verbal) and Fluid (nonverbal) score along with an IQ composite score.1
The Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, 4th Edition (WISC-IV)[5] Block Design subtest was used to measure visuo-spatial organization. The subtest is a core perceptual reasoning timed test that requires that the individual reproduce designs using blocks that have red surfaces, white surfaces, and surfaces divided diagonally into half red and half white. The Digit span forward subtest of the WISC-IV was used to assess rote learning and memory. The WISC-IV version is somewhat easier (as it is designed for children ages 6 to 16). It contains series ranging in length from two to nine digits and primarily involves rote learning and memory. Digit forward requires participants to recite a series of digits in the exact order given by the evaluator (forward). Conversely, Digit Span backwards requires participants to recite series of digits backwards and taps ability to manipulate verbal material in working memory.
Haxby Extension.[1] Like the WISC Block Design subtest, the Haxby Extension requires that participants reproduce designs using blocks with red surfaces, white surface, and surfaced divided diagonally into half red and half white. However, the designs are simpler than those on the WISC, such as starting with reproducing a single block. Possible cores range from 0-16.
The Beery-Buktenica Developmental Test of Visual Motor Integration (VMI)[6] involves the copying of figures of progressive complexity using paper and pencil. It is designed to assess the extent to which the individual can integrate his/her visual and motor abilities. Scores range from 0 to 30 (higher scores indicating more correctly copied items).
The Modified Cued Recall Test (mCRT)[7] is a list-learning task that measure verbal learning and memory. The mCRT consists of a training period in which 12 items are presented, 4 at a time, with each item accompanied by a unique category cue. The testing phase consists of 3 trials which generates a free recall score (FRS -spontaneous recall of the 12 items), cued recall score (CRS-items only recalled when the category cue is provided), a total score (sum FRS and CRS), and an intrusion score (items named that were not on the original list).
The Modified Cats and Dogs Task[8] is an adapted version of the Stroop Task and it assesses cognitive inhibition. Specifically, the task measures an individual’s ability to inhibit a natural response (naming pictures of a dog as “dog”) and to replace it with an incongruent one (naming pictures of a dog as “cat”).
The Purdue Pegboard Test[9] measures manual dexterity (left and right hand separately) as a participant places pins/pegs in a pegboard with 2 parallel rows.
The Cancellation Task-Revised[10] measures processing speed, visual selective attention, vigilance, perceptual speed and visuomotor ability by asking participants to circle a target stimulus/picture on a page containing both target pictures and distractor pictures.
The Tinetti Assessment Tool: Gait subtest[11] quantifies gait in 7 domains by observing participants while walking. Those domains are: Initiation of Gait, Step Length, Step Height/ Foot Clearance, Step Symmetry, Step Continuity, Path, Trunk and Walking Stance.

Caregiver Interview Measures

The Dementia Questionnaire for People with Learning Disabilities (DLD).[12] The DLD consists of 50 items in 8 subscales, including short-term memory, long-term memory spatial and temporal orientation, speech, practical skills, mood, activity and interest, and behavioral disturbances. The DLD generates two scores, one reflecting cognitive abilities (DLD/SCS) and one reflecting social skills (DLD/SOS).
The NTG Early Detection Screen for Dementia (NTG-EDSD).[13] The NTG-EDSD includes sections on behavioral/cognitive domains that could reflect indications of dementia clinical progression. In the current ABC-DS cohort, the DLD and NTG significantly differentiated participants who were Unaffected from those with MCI-DS and Dementia.
The Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales, 3rd edition (VABS-3),[14] Domain Level Parent/ Caregiver Form, provides a standardized assessment of functional abilities for use with individuals (ages 0 – 90+) having intellectual and developmental disabilities. The Domain Level Parent/Caregiver Form is administered as a semi-structured interview with the parent or caregiver and focuses on home and family- life behavior. Adaptive functioning within three domains, Communication, Daily Living Skills, and Socialization, are assessed.1 2
The Reiss Screen for Maladaptive Behavior[15] was created specifically for use with individuals with ID to screen for possible depression, psychosis and behavior problems. Caregivers are asked to rate 38 symptoms of psychiatric behavior as being “No Problem”, “Problem”, or “Major Problem” for the individual in question. Behaviors are categorized into 8 sub-domains reflecting (a) aggressive behavior, autistic-like behavior, (c) psychosis, (d) paranoia, (e)/(f) behavioral and physical symptoms of depression, (g) dependent personality disorder, and (h) avoidant disorder.
The Neuropsychiatric Inventory (NPI)[16] is a validated instrument used to assess neuropsychiatric symptoms in dementia. The questionnaire asks caregivers to retrospectively rate symptoms in 12 neuropsychiatric domains: delusions, hallucinations, agitation/ aggression, dysphoria/depression, anxiety, euphoria/elation, apathy/indifference, disinhibition, irritability/lability, aberrant motor behaviors, nighttime behavioral disturbances, and appetite/ eating disturbance.2

Health Status and Life Events

Demographics Questionnaire; includes year of participant’s birth; sex; ethnicity and race; primary language; age of mother and of father at participant’s birth; handedness; primary residence; level of independence; activities (e.g., vocational training program, working in the community, postsecondary education program).
Medical Health History Questionnaire; includes Psychiatric conditions (e.g., depressive disorder, anxiety disorder); Neurological conditions (e.g., dementia, stroke, seizure disorder); Eyes/ears/nose/throat (e.g., cataracts, visual impairment, hearing impairment); Cardiovascular conditions (e.g., congenital heart disease, pacemaker); Respiratory conditions (e.g., asthma, pneumonia, sleep apnea); Allergies; Dermatologic/Connective tissue conditions (e.g., alopecia, rosacea); Musculoskeletal conditions (e.g., osteoporosis; gout); Endocrine/metabolic conditions (e.g., diabetes, Vitamin B12 deficiency); Menstrual information (e.g., menopausal); Hormone replacement information; Gastrointestinal conditions (e.g., celiac disease, irritable bowel syndrome); Hematopoetic conditions (e.g., anemia); Cancer; Surgery; Covid-19; and Other conditions;
Stress Index: Caregivers were asked about the presence or absence of significant life events and were asked to rate how the participant handled the experience.2

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
section: 1.5 Special Cautions When Using this Dataset
text:
1.5 Special Cautions When Using this Dataset
Modified Cued Recall Test. Two sets of stimuli images (Set A/ Version 1 or Set B/ Version 2) were used. The set used varied across site and data collection cycle. Analyses conducted thus far suggest that set/version administered influences participant performance. The following variables are unique to the ABC-DS U19 study: crset, ift1r, ift2r, ift3r, tifrreps, agfrin1, agfrin2, agfrin3, tafi, c1t1, c1t2, c1t3, c2t1, c2t2, c2t3, c3t1, c3t2, c3t3, crc1, crc2 and crc3.
Down Syndrome Mental Status Examination (DSMSE): A component of the DSMSE, the Shoebox Memory Test, was unique to the ADDS U01 and ABC-DS U19 studies; thus, sample sizes for the colshbx and dsto2 will be smaller than those for other DSMSE variables. Similarly, variables colshbxtime, dsme1time, colmemscr and nonmemory are unique to the ABC-DS U19 study; thus, sample sizes for these variables will be smaller than those for other variables.
Modified Cats and Dogs Test: The variables, nttrain and strain, are new and unique to the ABC-DS U19 study.
NTG-Early Detection Screen for Dementia (NTG-EDSD): The Caregiver Concern Questions, variables ntspcon and ntspconage, are unique to the ADDS U01 and ABC-DS U19 studies.
Purdue Pegboard Test: Variables pp_dh_tr and pp_ndh_tr are unique to the ABC-DS U19 study. The variable de_handedness can be used to determine participants’ dominant hand (1 = Right-handed, 2 = Left-handed, 3 = Ambidextrous).
Stress Index: The variable si_victim is a new variable unique to the ABC-DS U19 study.
Tinetti Gait Assessment: The following variables are unique to the ABC-DS U19 study: tbagait, tbaslh, tbafc, tbass, tbasc, tbap, tbat, and tbawt.
Verbal Fluency- McCarthy Category Fluency Test. The “Exceptional Scoring Method” is used. Variables vffdexepr and vfanexepr are unique to the ADDS U01 and ABC-DS U19 studies; the variable vftotexepr is unique to the ABC-DS U19 study.

  Footnotes
  
    Baseline Only
    Data of this type not collected in all three studies; N may be lower than for other measures.

title: 1  Clinical Core Methods
text:
1.6 Protocol for Sibling Controls
Approximately 50 sibling controls were recruited and underwent the same procedures with the exception of no caregiver assessments and a small cognitive screening battery. In addition, during the 2015-2020 U01 grant, only baseline blood draws were conducted. Sibling controls provided medical histories and were screened using the AD8[17] (a brief informant interview to detect dementia) and the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA).[18] The two screens were used to simply ensure that the siblings did not have MCI/Dementia and are not included in the ABC-DS database.




1. Haxby JV. Neuropsychological evaluation of adults with down’s syndrome: Patterns of selective impairment in non-demented old adults. Journal of Intellectual Disability Research. 1989;33(3):193-210.


2. McCarthy D. MSCA: McCarthy Scales of Children’s Abilities. Psychological Corporation; 1972.


3. Korkman M, Kirk U, Kemp S. Nepsy-(nepsy-ii). Published online 2007.


4. Kaufman AS. Kaufman brief intelligence test–second edition (KBIT-2). Circle Pines, MN: American Guidance Service. Published online 2004.


5. Wechsler D. WISC-IV. Wechsler intelligence scale for children–fourth UK edition. Administration and scoring manual. Published online 2003.


6. Beery KE. Beery VMI: The beery-buktenica developmental test of visual-motor integration. Minneapolis, MN: Pearson. Published online 2004.


7. Devenny D, Zimmerli E, Kittler P, Krinsky-McHale S. Cued recall in early-stage dementia in adults with down’s syndrome. Journal of Intellectual Disability Research. 2002;46(6):472-483.


8. Ball SL, Holland AJ, Treppner P, Watson PC, Huppert FA. Executive dysfunction and its association with personality and behaviour changes in the development of alzheimer’s disease in adults with down syndrome and mild to moderate learning disabilities. British Journal of Clinical Psychology. 2008;47(1):1-29.


9. Tiffin J, Asher EJ. The purdue pegboard: Norms and studies of reliability and validity. Journal of applied psychology. 1948;32(3):234.


10. Krinsky-McHale SJ, Devenny DA, Kittler P, Silverman W. Selective attention deficits associated with mild cognitive impairment and early stage alzheimer’s disease in adults with down syndrome. American Journal on Mental Retardation. 2008;113(5):369-386.


11. Tinetti ME. Performance-oriented assessment of mobility problems in elderly patients. Journal of the American Geriatrics Society. Published online 1986.


12. Eurlings H, Evenhuis H, Kenegen M. Dementia questionnaire for people with learning disabilities (DLD). UK adaptation. Published online 2006.


13. Esralew L, Janicki MP, Keller SM. National task group early detection screen for dementia (NTG-EDSD). In: Neuropsychological Assessments of Dementia in down Syndrome and Intellectual Disabilities. Springer; 2017:197-213.


14. Sparrow S, Saulnier C, Cicchetti D. Vineland adaptive behavior scales (3rd ed.). Published online 2016.


15. Reiss S. Handbook of Challenging Behavior: Mental Health Aspects of Mental Retardation. Ids Pub.; 1994.


16. Cummings JL, Mega M, Gray K, Rosenberg-Thompson S, Carusi DA, Gornbein J. The neuropsychiatric inventory: Comprehensive assessment of psychopathology in dementia. Neurology. 1994;44(12):2308-2308.


17. Galvin J, Roe C, Powlishta K, et al. The AD8: A brief informant interview to detect dementia. Neurology. 2005;65(4):559-564.


18. Nasreddine ZS, Phillips NA, Bédirian V, et al. The montreal cognitive assessment, MoCA: A brief screening tool for mild cognitive impairment. Journal of the American Geriatrics Society. 2005;53(4):695-699.

title: 2  Genomic Data
text:
2.1 Genotyping
Last Revised: October 10, 2024
VERSION 1.0
GENOMIC DATA (GENOME-WIDE SNP MICROARRAY)
Authors Lam-Ha T. Dang1, M. Ilyas Kamboh2, Carlos Cruchaga3, and Joseph H. Lee1
Affiliations
Summary. This dataset includes genome-wide microarray data for 373 adults with Down Syndrome (DS) and 43 sibling non-DS controls (total N=416) in the Alzheimer’s Biomarkers Consortium — Down Syndrome (ABC-DS) study. This genomic data set includes data on study participants who were enrolled in the U01 part of the ABC-DS study as of 4/30/2022.
Genotyping was performed at the Center for Applied Genomics at Children’s Hospital of Pennsylvania using the Illumina Infinium General Screening Array (GSA) Version 2, with added disease markers for all samples, except for 25 participants for which Version 3 was used. In total, 759,993 and 654,027 variants across the genome were genotyped on GSAv2 and GSAv3, respectively. The two versions of the genotyping microarray had 619,856 (~95%) overlapping variants. We have provided several subsets of the complete data set (see “Summary of the data files provided” for more details). Variants in these data were mapped to the GRCh37/hg19 assembly.

title: 2  Genomic Data
text:
2.2 Trisomic Chromosome 21
Chromosome 21 was treated separately because traditional tools cannot be applied to trisomic chromosome 21. Trisomic variants were treated as copy number variants (CNV) and were called in using the cnvPartition CNV Analysis (v3.2.0) plug-in in GenomeStudio 2.0 with Genotyping module.[1,2] Allele specific genotypes were then exported using the CNV Region Report plug-in (v.2.1.2)[2]. In total, 9,785 and 8,446 variants were genotyped on GSAv2 and GSAv3, respectively. As done commonly, the p-arm of Chr. 21 was dropped from analysis because it is known to be highly heterochromatic with numerous repeat sequences. In the current data, this characteristic resulted in high rate of variants called as disomic in among those karyotyped with DS and discrepancies between duplicates in this region. For these reasons, all variants genotyped on the p-arm should be excluded from analysis.

title: 2  Genomic Data
text:
2.3 Genotype quality control
To maximize the flexibility of these data for all investigators, we aimed to minimize the likelihood of distributing erroneous data, while minimizing the quality control steps. For example, we included SNPs that did not achieve high genotyping rates, which would have been dropped in a standard analysis practice. Specifically, we checked for sex-mismatch cases by identifying cases where sex obtained from the analysis of X-chromosome SNPs using PLINK[3] did not match the reported sex. These cases were re-confirmed with research coordinators to rule out potential data entry error, etc. Relationships (e.g., siblings, monozygotic twins) among subjects were confirmed by pair-wise genome-wide estimates of proportion identity-by-descent (IBD) computed using the KING[4] software. Discrepant relationships were reviewed against available demographic and family data (when siblings are genotyped) to determine the most likely relationship consistent with the proportion of IBD or other scenarios (i.e., sample duplicates, duplicate enrollments across cohorts). Samples with unresolved or ambiguous identities arising from mismatched sex and discrepant relationships were excluded from the dataset. In duplicated samples, the samples with the lower genotyping call rate were dropped from the data set. In the end, data were provided for 416 participants.

title: 2  Genomic Data
text:
2.4 Imputation
We are not providing imputed data and suggest the TOPMed Imputation Server for genotype imputation.

title: 2  Genomic Data
text:
2.5 Summary of the data files
Nomenclature Subject Identification (ID) Number

For non-DS control siblings, the Individual ID (IID) of the DS-proband was used as the Family ID (FID)
For families consisting of multiple participants with DS, the lower valued IID was used as the FID
In data files related to Chromosome 21, participant IDs were formatted as follows: “FID_IID”

Trisomic Chromosome 21 genotypes

ABC-DS.LONI.Chr21.GRCh37.CNCounts.txt: Copy number count at each genotyped position.
ABC-DS.LONI.Chr21.GRCh37.Dosage.txt: Dosage of the “A” allele (“A_Allele” column). Dosage can be 0, 1, 2, or 3 copies.
ABC-DS.LONI.Chr21.GRCh37.Genotype.txt: Genotype in ACGT coding

Non-Chromosome 21 genotypes: These genotypes are provided in binary PLINK data files (i.e., bed/bim/fam files) with pedigree and sex information.
PLINK files consisting of all variants genotyped (i.e., the union of GSAv2 and GSAv3 chips)

ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.allSNPs.bed
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.allSNPs.bim
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.allSNPs.fam

PLINK files consisting of only overlapping variants (i.e., the intersection) between GSAv2 and GSAv3 chips

ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.bed
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.bim
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.fam

PLINK files consisting of the remaining non-overlapping variants

ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.nonOverlappingVars.bed
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.nonOverlappingVars.bim
ABC-DS.GRCh37.non-Chr21.LONI.nonOverlappingVars.fam

About the Authors: This document was prepared by Lam-Ha T. Dang and Joseph H. Lee. For more information please contact Joseph H. Lee and CC: Lam-Ha T. Dang.
Notice: This document is presented by the author(s) as a service to ABC-DS data users. However, users should be aware that no formal review process has vetted this document and that ABC-DS cannot guarantee the accuracy or utility of this document




1. Illumina, Inc. GenomeStudio. Published online 2020. https://www.illumina.com/techniques/microarrays/array-data-analysis-experimental-design/genomestudio.html


2. Illumina, Inc. GenomeStudio 2.0 plug-in. Published online 2020. https://support.illumina.com/downloads/genomestudio-2-0-plug-ins.html


3. Chang CC, Chow CC, Tellier LCAM, Vattikuti S, Purcell SM, Lee JJ. Second-generation PLINK: Rising to the challenge of larger and richer datasets. GigaScience. 2015;4:7. doi:10.1186/s13742-015-0047-8


4. Manichaikul A, Mychaleckyj JC, Rich SS, Daly K, Sale M, Chen WM. Robust relationship inference in genome-wide association studies. Bioinformatics. 2010;26(22):2867-2873. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btq559

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.1 Overview
Last Revised: October 10, 2024
VERSION 1.0

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.1.1 Database Structure


What is the overall structure of the database?


The research design calls for evaluating each subject at intervals of approximately 16 months. The data available on LONI are organized into separate tables, each representing and named for a particular data domain, e.g. Neuropsychiatric_Inventory, Registration, Premorbid Functioning. For data collected at each evaluation, tables are organized in a one-record-per-evaluation format, sorted by ID (subject_label) and consecutive evaluation number (event_sequence). The latency in days between the baseline evaluation and each subsequent evaluation, and the age of the subject at each evaluation are documented in the table Age_at_Event_and Latency. Data types collected only once for each subject have one record per subject_label. Tables contain data collected from control subjects have the suffix ‘_Control’ in the table name. All other data tables contain data collected from subjects with Down Syndrome.





3.1.2 Data Tables on LONI


How are data tables organized on the LONI website?


As shown in the table below, data are organized by domain. After selecting a domain, all tables in that category are shown; individual tables or all tables may be selected and downloaded.








3.1.3 Documentation


Is there documentation for each domain, in addition to information provided in this FAQ document?


Yes, a document with information specific to each domain has provided by project leaders responsible for the collection and processing of each data type. These documents appear in each of the domain-specific tabs on the LONI website.





3.1.4 Data Sources


What is the source of available data?


The data available on LONI come from three different projects: ADDS and NiAD, funded through a U01 mechanism, and the ongoing ABC-DS U19 project. The available data have been harmonized across all three projects. ADDS and NiAD collected non-overlapping samples of Down Syndrome cases. The current U19 project includes subjects continuing from the U01 projects as well as newly recruited subjects, i.e. individuals not previously seen in ADDS or NiAD. ADDS did not collect data on controls, while NiAD and the current U19 project recruited sibling controls of Down Syndrome subjects.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.2 Data Dictionary

3.2.1 Dictionary and Codebook


Is a data dictionary available?


Yes, a data dictionary containing the names and descriptions is available. For categorical variables, labels of each numeric value for each variable are provided in a separate codebook.





3.2.2 Free Text Columns


In the data dictionary, what are the field_types “D” “N” and “T”?


There are free text columns in the EDC, but they are omitted from the external investigator-facing data freezes.





3.2.3 Event Sequence


What does ‘event sequence’ refer to?


Event_sequence is just that, an indication of the sequential number of times the subject has had across the ABC-DS study.





3.2.4 Seizure Variable


Where is the ‘seizure’ variable in the harmonized dataset?


We no longer administer the health questions on the NTG-EDSD form in the U19, so the seizure variable wouldn’t be in the harmonized data set (U01/U19). However, there is a variable in the health history form (hh_seizure). This will only tell us if there has ever been a seizure diagnosis. It does not tell us whether it is currently an issue. We had to collapse the Recent/Active and Remote/Inactive variables into just yes/no in the harmonized data set.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.3 Cognitive and Caregiver Measures

3.3.1 Cognitive Inventory


Is there an inventory of which neuro/psych/cognitive tests are available for ABC-DS participants?


Yes, you will find these in the Clinical Core Methods Document posted on LONI or available here





3.3.2 Vineland Data


There is only one assessment per subject and the event_sequence takes different values depending on the subject. Does this imply this assessment was only collected once per subject and possibly at different visits (not necessarily at baseline)?


Each participant only has one Vineland record. It might have been done in a visit that was not the Baseline visit.





3.3.3 ABC-DS IQ Data


The variables MTAGE1, MTAGE2, MTAGE3 in the ABC-DS IQ Worksheet data do not seem to be included in the documentation provided. Q: Can you please point to any data specifications where these variables are described.


Not many participants had alternate additional sources. “MTAGE” stands for Mental Age for source 1, 2, and 3.





3.3.4 Cognitive Distributions


Are all the distributions normal for the cognitive tests?


For all the cognitive measures, before performing any modelling, please check the variables distributions. The vast majority of them have a skewed distributions and are zero inflated, which requires either some type of transformation or proper modelling.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.4 Adverse Events


Do you know if we can find what adverse events were recorded?


The only AE data in the harmonized dataset is cause of death if occurred.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.5 NeuroImaging

3.5.1 MRI Scans


Is it possible to see MRI reports or a list with the # and type of abnormal findings, e.g., areas of siderosis or micro-/macro-hemorrhages?


This data is not available in the harmonized dataset.





3.5.2 Image Processing


I was not able to view or add post-processed images to my data collection and also wondered what kind of pre-processing and post-processing was done.


Our Neuroimaging Core leads are Brad Christian and Adam Brickman. You can contact them for more information. In the future there will be a Methods document available on LONI for Neuroimaging.





3.5.3 Structural MRI Scans


Can you provide any information on decisions about which T1 sequences were used at which sites / visits. The dataset contains several variations of MPRAGE and SPGR sequences, and we want to ensure that our analyses are not biased in any way by the different types of sequences.


The specific acquisition parameters should be contained within the header information for each scan. For the U19 data, T1-weighted scans were collected using the ADNI 4 protocol, set up by the Mayo group for the different scanners (I think most are on Siemens, maybe one or two sites are on GE). Prior to the U19, there were two separate consortia (we sometimes call this the “U01 data”), including NiAD & ADDS. The T1-weighted scan parameters were harmonized within consortium but not necessarily across consortia.





3.5.4 Available Imaging Results


What analysis results data is available on LONI?


The only analysis results currently at LONI are those for centiloid for the former NiAD/ADDS study.





3.5.5 Centiloid


How does the centiloid file interact with the event_sequence?


Currently it does not include the event_sequence and it is all “U01” data. In the future the event_sequence will be added.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.6 Clinically Significant Findings


Does ABCDS-U19 ClinSigFindings 2 include all abnormal MRI findings from the U19 cohort?


Yes, it contains all the findings that were reported and entered into the EDC by the time of the data freeze Health History and Concurrent Medications.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.7 Health History and Medications


Is there any information on concurrent medications and prior medical history in Freeze 4 data?


Yes, these tables are available on LONI.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.8 Lookup Table


Is there a look up table for all these IDs?


Yes, the ‘crosswalk’ is in the internal analytic file, available to ABC-DS internal investigators





Is there an indicator of whether or not a participant is on TRC-DS since treatments can alter biomarker results?


Yes, we know if they were/are co-enrolled, but that doesn’t mean they are in a clinical trial (yet); the eCRF/data for clinical trial participation is in progress as of 9/2024.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.9 Harmonization


Is imaging and IQ data harmonized between ADDS and NiAD?


For imaging, it depends on the measure; amyloid PET is harmonized using centiloids; MRI and Tau PET are not harmonized. If amyloid PET is used for analyses then centiloids should be used since centiloids are a standardized measure of amyloid deposition. Site should still be included in all analysis as a covariate regardless of harmonized data or not. All derived MRI measures (cortical thickness and volumetrics) as well as Tau PET are not harmonized and at least Combat should be employed for derived imaging measures to derive harmonized outcomes. For voxel level analysis some standardization can be employed, such as RAVEL, but it is generally variable. Reach out to Biostats Core Dr. Tudorascu to discuss a harmonization plan if needed. Some useful references.[1–3] For IQ, there is a premorbid functioning level variable prefunclevel.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.10 Clinical Lab and MOMs Data


Are clinical chemistry data (e.g., cholesterol) included?


Yes, this data is a standalone file on LONI





Are MOMs study data included?


Not yet, there will be a separate MOMs data file when the supplement ends in December 2024.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.11 Gap


How was the gap between the visit and scanning/assay dealt with?


There are latency variables between all events

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.12 Errors


How are the data entry errors or sample switches handled?


LONI has a procedure





Is there a website so that data users know whether they are using the updated and correct data?


In the ABC-DS Project in LONI, the current data freeze is available; track the dates of your datasets; check the Ns;

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.13 Latency and Event Sequence

3.13.1 Event Sequence


Can you please confirm the variable event_sequence == 1 refers to baseline and clinical_latency_in_days can be used to determine the time since baseline for all visits with event_sequence > 1.


Yes, confirmed.





3.13.2 Latency


What are the latency variables?


clinical_latency_in_days; amy_latency_in_days; tau_latency_in_days; fdg_latency_in_days; csf_latency_in_days; all use Cycle 1/baseline as the reference point.

title: 3  Frequently Asked Questions
text:
3.14 Biospecimens/Omics

3.14.1 Biospeciman Dataset


How is the event sequence determined in the biospecimen dataset? Can we use rundate to determine the baseline visit? It seems that rundate may not be valid since it does not reflect the time of sample collection. In this case, how do we then determine baseline, and the relative timing (in days, months) of subsequent assessments?


The “Biospecimens” file should not be used, it was deleted in late August 2024, Use the CSF file.





3.14.2 Proteomics


Where is the proteomic file?


It is not on LONI yet. As of October 2024 we are waiting for the Methods document before the data can be posted.
